title: Meet The Parents

jokes.text: Surprise parties are tacky and far too often organized by well-intentioned people with very bad taste. But Patrick has always wanted one, poor thing. So I am throwing him a surprise party tonight.
jokes.setup: Moira opens with a sweeping condemnation of surprise parties as tacky and poorly organized.
jokes.punchline: She immediately announces she is throwing one, with the pitying 'poor thing' treating Patrick's desire as an unfortunate affliction.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly Moira-coded opening: she establishes her superiority, dismisses the concept, then does the thing anyway while maintaining the moral high ground. 'Poor thing' is the key word — she pities Patrick for wanting something she finds gauche, yet indulges him. Strong character integration; only Moira can thread this needle.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Surprise parties are tacky and organized by people with bad taste.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm throwing one.

jokes.text: When I planned that Casablanca-themed party for your mother's 40th, I had to quarantine the camels for a month.
jokes.setup: Johnny is gently pushing back on David's last-minute planning.
jokes.punchline: The specific, insane detail of quarantining camels for a month — implying the Roses actually had live camels at a birthday party — lands as a perfect glimpse into their former excess.
jokes.explanation: Extraordinary economy — one throwaway dependent clause does all the work. The quarantine detail is the key: it implies a logistical bureaucracy around camels that the Roses treated as totally routine. It's worldbuilding, character comedy, and a laugh all in one sentence. Highly quotable and rewards rewatching as a window into the Rose family's absurd former life.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We'll need to quarantine the camels for at least two weeks before the party.

jokes.text: I've actually been organizing this for quite some time. I'm just telling you right now because I know that none of you can keep a secret.
jokes.setup: The family criticizes David's last-minute notice for the party.
jokes.punchline: David flips the accusation — he's not disorganized, he's strategically withheld information because his family are gossips.
jokes.explanation: A solid comeback that immediately gets proven right. The structure of 'accusation → logical rebuttal that doubles as an insult' is clean. Works well because it simultaneously defends David and attacks the family.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have been planning. I just don't trust any of you with secrets. Moira once told me she was keeping a surprise party for me, and then showed up to my birthday in a mermaid costume.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It was a themed party.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: My birthday isn't under the sea.

jokes.text: Oh! At 9 a.m. it's a little early for character assassination.
jokes.setup: David has just told the family he can't trust them with secrets.
jokes.punchline: Moira's objection isn't that David is wrong — it's that the timing is inappropriate. The implicit acknowledgment that later in the day she'd accept it.
jokes.explanation: The joke hinges on Moira not actually denying she can't keep a secret — she only objects to the hour. It's a very specific Moira mode: wounded dignity that accidentally proves the point. Highly quotable line, excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're all terrible at keeping secrets.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Character assassination! This is character assassination!

jokes.text: Not one of you knows how the 'Crows' movie ends. / You told me the day you got home. / You gave me the script.
jokes.setup: Moira invokes her secret-keeping record as defense.
jokes.punchline: Both Alexis and Johnny immediately, separately, and specifically cite how Moira spoiled the Crows movie for each of them — Alexis verbally, Johnny via the physical script.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured rapid-fire double confirmation. Moira sets herself up, and two different characters immediately dismantle her claim with two distinct specific methods — one auditory, one physical. The escalation from 'told me' to 'gave me the script' is perfect comic engineering. Then Moira's response doubles down further.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We can't keep secrets.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Evidence: they revealed the ending of 'The Crows' movie.

jokes.text: And you were both sworn to secrecy, so David is right not to trust you.
jokes.setup: Both Alexis and Johnny have just proven Moira spoiled the Crows movie for them.
jokes.punchline: Moira pivots instantly: rather than accepting blame, she reframes the scandal so that the children are the betrayers of her confidence. Perfectly self-serving logic.
jokes.explanation: Moira's rhetorical judo is peak character comedy. She transforms being caught in a secret-spilling into a morality lecture about loyalty. The logic is airtight within Moira's worldview, which is what makes it hilarious. Strong character integration — this is uniquely Moira.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You spoiled the movie for me!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, I didn't tell anyone.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, we would never do that.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh really? Because I remember you telling me about it the second you got home, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And you, Alexis—I literally gave you the script to read.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I thought that was confidential!

jokes.text: Yes, things are certainly racing along at a dangerously rapid velocity.
jokes.setup: Johnny enthusiastically reacts to David inviting Patrick's parents, calling it 'a big step.'
jokes.punchline: Moira's overwrought, polysyllabic way of saying 'this is moving fast' — the vocabulary is excessive but the underlying concern is identical to what a normal parent might say.
jokes.explanation: A reliable Moira verbal tic — saying something completely mundane in the most baroque possible diction. Works well, though the joke type is well-established by this point in the series. Character integration is excellent; economy is intentionally poor, which is the gag.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David would never betray a confidence. He's very good at keeping secrets. In fact, I didn't even know he was in a movie until he told me about it last week.

jokes.text: Thank you so much, we're doing great.
jokes.setup: Moira has expressed concern that things are 'racing along at a dangerously rapid velocity.'
jokes.punchline: David responds as if Moira asked a normal 'how are you' question, refusing to engage with the subtext and instead projecting cheerful confidence — a defensive non-answer.
jokes.explanation: David's brittle social defense mechanism in miniature — respond to anxiety with aggressive cheerfulness. Five words that do a lot of character work. Clean and efficient.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have one more surprise for you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've invited your parents.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They're here. They're waiting outside.

jokes.text: Now, I will only be throwing one surprise party, and one surprise party only. So tonight there will be a surprise. And there will be tears. Even if they're just my own.
jokes.setup: Moira asserts control of the party logistics.
jokes.punchline: The escalating self-martyrdom culminates in Moira preemptively threatening to cry regardless of how the party goes — making it about herself.
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira: a heartfelt gesture framed entirely around the possibility of her own emotional experience. 'Even if they're just my own' is the perfect button — an admission that she may be the only one moved, delivered with total dignity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, one moment you're meeting their parents, and the next moment you're buying a timeshare in Scottsdale and arguing about whose turn it is to use the bidet.

jokes.text: How did it end again? Like, a crowmageddon? / Crowpocalypse. But you didn't hear it from me.
jokes.setup: The scene is wrapping up; Alexis casually asks about the Crows movie ending.
jokes.punchline: Moira corrects the title (Crowpocalypse), then immediately swears Alexis to secrecy — completing the callback loop that proves she still can't stop herself from spoiling it.
jokes.explanation: The best structural joke in the cold open. The entire preceding sequence has established Moira can't keep secrets, and in the scene's final breath she proves it again — correcting 'crowmageddon' to 'crowpocalypse' with precise confidence, then adding 'but you didn't hear it from me.' The disclaimer is the punchline: she has no self-awareness that she just did the thing. Beautifully earned, high rewatch value, and the 'crowpocalypse' vs 'crowmageddon' distinction is itself funny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This will be special.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've planned something you're not expecting.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I hope you're ready to be surprised.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm not sure I'm ready for this.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is harder than I thought it would be.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know why I'm getting emotional about this.

jokes.text: This is a really big deal, Johnny. I mean, meeting my best friend's son's boyfriend's parents for the very first time. I am very touched that you wanted me to share this moment with you. / I asked you to mow the lawn three times, Roland. So you being here is more your decision than mine.
jokes.setup: Roland frames his presence at a significant family moment as an honored invitation.
jokes.punchline: Johnny reveals Roland was repeatedly asked to do yard work and simply showed up anyway — reframing Roland's emotional speech as an act of avoidance.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful setup/punchline with perfect character specificity. Roland's overwrought sentiment ('best friend's son's boyfriend's parents') is funny on its own, but Johnny's flat correction is the real joke. The relational chain Roland builds is also quietly comic. Strong Johnny/Roland dynamic payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so what happens at the end?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I don't want to spoil it for you, but let's just say it's a real... Freddy Krueger meets Jaws situation. A Fredaws, if you will.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's more of a Nightmare on Elm Street meets The Ring type thing. A Nightmare Ring. Or a Ringtmare, really.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: But I've already said too much. The studios really don't want me talking about this.

jokes.text: You must be David's Dad. / Oh, no, no! I do get that all the time, though. It's uh, you know, you've got David's handsome looks there, and then my face.
jokes.setup: Clint Brewer assumes Roland is David's father based on appearance.
jokes.punchline: Roland confirms he's NOT David's dad, but then gives a physical description that implies he considers himself ugly compared to David — delivered completely without self-pity.
jokes.explanation: Roland's cheerful self-deprecation ('you've got David's handsome looks there, and then my face') works because he delivers it without shame. The premise that Clint would confuse Roland for David's father is also subtly funny. Good character integration — Roland's oblivious bonhomie shines.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, I'm really touched by this moment. You all mean so much to me.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You're like... well, you're my former business associates who are now my neighbors, but also kind of my friends, except we've had that thing where we weren't speaking for a bit, but now we're past that, so I guess you're more like... acquaintances who've become closer through proximity and shared experiences?

jokes.text: Now, Patrick tells me you run the motel. / More like, runs it into the ground.
jokes.setup: Clint makes polite small talk about Johnny's work.
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately and cheerfully undercuts Johnny with a casual insult, seemingly oblivious to social propriety.
jokes.explanation: A quick Roland gag — he's constitutionally incapable of being a wingman. The 'runs it into the ground' pun (running → running into the ground) is decent wordplay. Economy is excellent. Doesn't add much new characterization but lands in the moment.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: This is nice, Johnny. I appreciate you thinking of me for this special moment.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, you're not here because I invited you. You're here because Jazzagals is doing their spring cleaning today.

jokes.text: Oh, we do have a lot of fun around here, don't we? / We do, sometimes.
jokes.setup: Roland tries to smooth over his insult by invoking camaraderie.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's grudging 'we do, sometimes' — not quite agreeing, not quite denying — perfectly captures his exasperated tolerance of Roland.
jokes.explanation: A small but real laugh — the 'sometimes' qualifier does the work. Johnny's begrudging acknowledgment of any positive Roland experience is inherently funny. Functional character beat, not a standout.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, we're both very handsome men. It's an easy mistake to make.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: David's just got better bones, better skin, better hair, better everything really. But other than that, we're practically twins.

jokes.text: I've been somewhat of a skeptic about those two. / This one isn't as progressive as the rest of us. It's a generational thing with him. / I was talking about the store, Roland.
jokes.setup: Clint Brewer reveals he's been skeptical about David and Patrick. Roland immediately assumes he means Patrick being gay.
jokes.punchline: Johnny clarifies he meant the business relationship — revealing multiple layers of irony: Roland assumed homophobia, Johnny corrects to business concerns, but the audience now suspects Clint's parents may not know Patrick is gay at all.
jokes.explanation: Elegant misdirection that does double duty: Roland's assumption is funny on its own (he immediately goes to the most charged interpretation), and Johnny's correction ('I was talking about the store, Roland') is funny as a deadpan correction. But the scene is doing something more — planting the episodic mystery about whether the Brewers know Patrick is gay. High rewatch bonus because this moment takes on new meaning once you know the twist.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, he runs it into the ground, if that's what you mean.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I've seen better management at a lemonade stand.

jokes.text: The Brewers' reaction shot when they realize Patrick hasn't told them about being gay — frozen smiles, visible processing.
jokes.setup: Johnny has brought up the romantic relationship between David and Patrick.
jokes.punchline: The Brewers' faces betray that this is news to them — an implied visual freeze that the dialogue then confirms ('it kind of seems like the two of you just found out some really shocking information').
jokes.explanation: A purely visual/reactive comedic beat that the subsequent dialogue confirms and narrates. The structural device of having Roland be the one to name what just happened ('it kind of seems like they didn't know') is a classic sitcom move. Strong cringe potential.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I think that's great, Roland. I really do. And I'm sure that once people get to know me, they'll see past all of that and realize what a tremendous asset I could be to this community.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, well... you're not *completely* useless, I guess.

jokes.text: Did I speak out of turn? / I-I think we'll just go to the room, and get ready for the big surprise tonight.
jokes.setup: The Brewers have visibly reacted to learning Patrick and David are romantically involved.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's understated 'Did I speak out of turn?' followed by the Brewers' hasty, performatively cheerful retreat is cringe comedy in miniature.
jokes.explanation: The understated 'did I speak out of turn' works as self-aware comedy — Johnny knows he's caused a problem but phrases it with maximum gentleness. The Brewers' retreat is the cringe beat. Functional but not a standout moment.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I just have some concerns about the two of them going into business together.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, you know, I get it. Two guys, it's not for everybody.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, that's not what I meant. I'm talking about the business venture.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Right, right. Two guys in business together. I hear you.

jokes.text: Huh. Correct me if I'm wrong here, Johnny. But it kinda seems like they didn't know their son is in a relationship with David.
jokes.setup: The Brewers have just awkwardly retreated to their room after the revelation.
jokes.punchline: Roland, with characteristic bluntness, states the obvious in the most understated possible way — 'correct me if I'm wrong here' framing a conclusion that is glaringly self-evident.
jokes.explanation: Roland as oblivious narrator of his own chaos. 'Correct me if I'm wrong' is a classic setup for someone who is obviously right. Reliable Roland character beat, nothing transcendent.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You're not very progressive, are you?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm very progressive. I just think mixing business and romance is a recipe for disaster.

jokes.text: You're looking especially fresh and photogenic today, Mother. / Why thank you, Alexis. Perhaps it's my excellent news.
jokes.setup: Alexis opens with an unusually complimentary greeting to Moira, suggesting she wants something.
jokes.punchline: Moira accepts the compliment entirely at face value and attributes it to her own mood about good news — not recognizing the manipulation.
jokes.explanation: Standard Alexis manipulation setup, but Moira's response (attributing the compliment to her own inner state rather than Alexis's motive) is characterful. Functional scene-setter comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yeah, and you two looked real surprised when you found out. I mean, your jaws were on the ground. You didn't know they were business partners.

jokes.text: There's a darling little medical clinic in Elm Grove where if any one of us, say, you, or your father, participates in a few nugatory tests, we could be bringing in over $200 a week!
jokes.setup: Moira announces she's found a revenue stream for the Cabaret deficit.
jokes.punchline: The 'revenue stream' turns out to be clinical trials, delivered with the same enthusiasm Moira would use for a film deal — and she volunteers Alexis and Johnny first.
jokes.explanation: Moira volunteering her children for medical tests is darkly funny and character-perfect. 'Nugatory tests' is a great Moira word choice. The fact that she immediately offers up family members rather than herself is the core joke.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, that didn't go great.

jokes.text: Or, you could make $2,000 in one afternoon.
jokes.setup: Alexis has listened to Moira's medical trial scheme.
jokes.punchline: Alexis counters with a number five times higher, creating a comedic pause before she reveals it's a soap opera convention — not something illicit.
jokes.explanation: The gap before Alexis reveals what the $2,000 is for creates a brief misdirection beat — the audience wonders if it's something shady. Economical setup. Works as a transition joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, that color is really bringing out the sergeantatarms energy in your face.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Thank you, darling.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, so I was thinking, could you sign a permission slip for me?

jokes.text: Showing up, taking your picture with a motley crew of pale, dewy-faced salamander people. I haven't hit rock bottom quite yet. It's a matter of self-worth.
jokes.setup: Moira explains why she's avoided fan conventions in the past.
jokes.punchline: 'Pale, dewy-faced salamander people' is a wickedly specific description of dedicated soap opera fans, and claiming self-worth while broke in a motel in Schitt's Creek is deeply ironic.
jokes.explanation: 'Salamander people' is inspired — specific enough to feel observed, weird enough to feel distinctly Moira. The irony of invoking self-worth while in her current circumstances is rich. High quotability; 'salamander people' alone earns it. This is Moira at her characteristic best.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have some exciting news. I've found a promising new revenue stream for our family.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm going to be a test subject for medical trials.

jokes.text: You did a commercial for adult diapers! / In Japan! In full-face Kabuki makeup!
jokes.setup: Moira is refusing the fan convention on grounds of self-worth.
jokes.punchline: Alexis punctures the dignity claim by citing the diaper commercial; Moira's immediate defense — Japan! Kabuki! — makes it somehow funnier by being completely sincere.
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure is perfect: Alexis's one-line grenade destroys Moira's dignity argument, and Moira's three-part defense (Japan / Kabuki makeup) makes it exponentially worse. Each element Moira adds compounds the absurdity. 'In Japan!' is the comedy anchor — as if geography excuses everything. Highly quotable and rewatch-worthy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm offering comprehensive medical testing for only $200 a week.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, that's insane.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, if you'd prefer the premium package with bloodwork and imaging, that's $2,000 a week.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're running a medical scam out of our house?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I prefer to think of it as a wellness opportunity.

jokes.text: But there will be a safe word in case the gaggle of asymmetrical faces gets to be too much.
jokes.setup: Moira has agreed to attend the fan convention.
jokes.punchline: 'A gaggle of asymmetrical faces' as a description of fans is both elitist and bizarrely specific — and requesting a safe word implies the situation could become genuinely overwhelming.
jokes.explanation: Another Moira insult coin: 'asymmetrical faces' is specific enough to be funny and vague enough to be applicable to all of humanity. The safe word request is character-perfect — Moira treating fan interaction like a hostage situation. Builds on the 'salamander people' joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: They're like salamanders. Cold-blooded, slimy, and they reproduce by flinging their genetic material at each other indiscriminately.

jokes.text: I told Patrick that there's a salmonella outbreak at the cafe, so he will not be coming here until I tell him to tonight.
jokes.setup: David explains how he's keeping Patrick away from the party location.
jokes.punchline: Using a food safety health crisis as a party decoy — and specifically choosing salmonella — is disproportionately dramatic for the situation.
jokes.explanation: David's choice of lie is characteristically over-the-top — not a simple 'it's closed for cleaning' but a full disease outbreak. Stevie's immediate concern about health code violations is the better joke here.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have certain standards about my self-worth.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, you did a commercial for hemorrhoid cream.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It was a very tasteful commercial.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You were sitting on a bidet.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A French bidet. It was an art film.

jokes.text: Oh. Is it possible you could maybe... choose a lie that doesn't make the cafe look like it's in violation of health codes?
jokes.setup: David announces the salmonella lie.
jokes.punchline: Stevie doesn't object to the deception itself, only to the reputational damage — a perfectly pragmatic and character-specific response.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's pragmatism is her defining comic quality — she skips moral objection entirely and goes straight to business impact. 'I'm not against lying, just choose a better lie' is implied and character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I need a safe word for this gaggle of asymmetrical faces.

jokes.text: I think it's a pretty good one.
jokes.setup: Stevie has objected to the salmonella lie on practical grounds.
jokes.punchline: David's breezy, unironically confident 'I think it's a pretty good one' — he has no interest in her concern and genuinely believes the lie is clever.
jokes.explanation: Six words capturing David's selective self-confidence. Functional, small laugh. Better as a beat in a rhythm than a standalone joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I need you to stay away from the store tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Why? What's going on?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, the building is being treated for a very rare and aggressive form of mold. It's called... Fusarium Oxysporum Roseum. It's highly contagious and can cause severe respiratory damage, hair loss, and in some cases, temporary blindness.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The health inspector called this morning. We're completely quarantined until Thursday. You absolutely cannot come in. In fact, no one can. It's a biohazard situation.

jokes.text: Did you run here, Mr. Rose?
jokes.setup: Johnny arrives at the cafe, apparently out of breath, having just checked in the Brewers
jokes.punchline: Stevie's dry observation implies Johnny ran — his physical state visible to the audience and commented on with perfect Stevie understatement
jokes.explanation: A small visual gag inferred from context — Johnny's urgency has clearly shown physically. Stevie's one-line observation is efficient and character-true. Not a standout but it earns its place.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: So you lied about a salmonella outbreak to get out of a business meeting, and now the Rose Apothecary is closed, and we're hemorrhaging money.

jokes.text: What? They're not supposed to be here for another 11 minutes!
jokes.setup: Johnny casually mentions he just checked in the Brewers.
jokes.punchline: David's panic is expressed not in emotional terms but through schedule precision — '11 minutes' reveals the meticulous, anxiety-based planning beneath his confident exterior.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of '11 minutes' is the comedy — not 'another hour' or 'later.' It tells us David has a detailed itinerary and that even 11 minutes matters to him. Good character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Stevie, I made it. I'm here.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I can see that. You're sweating through your shirt and breathing like you just ran a marathon.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was in a hurry.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: To get here? Or to leave?

jokes.text: It's more how they reacted when I brought up the fact that you two were... romantically in business with each other.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to explain diplomatically that the Brewers seemed shocked.
jokes.punchline: In trying to combine 'romantically involved' and 'business partners,' he accidentally coins 'romantically in business with each other' — an unintentional mashup that perfectly captures his awkward circumspection.
jokes.explanation: A great piece of dialogue naturalism — Johnny's verbal stumble produces a phrase that's genuinely funny in its accidental accuracy. 'Romantically in business' is almost exactly what David and Patrick are, and the fact it sounds absurd reveals the absurdity of the situation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did you tell them about the blackout curtains? Because they need to be drawn by 6:47 PM. Not 6:45, not 6:50. 6:47.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because that's when the sun hits the west-facing window at the precise angle that causes a glare on the television screen, and I've timed it.

jokes.text: Ew!
jokes.setup: Johnny has just said 'romantically in business with each other.'
jokes.punchline: David's disgusted 'Ew!' — his signature verbal tic — deployed here as a response to the clunky phrase rather than the shocking news.
jokes.explanation: One word, maximum character. David's 'ew' is a series-signature response that here lands perfectly — his instinctive recoil from his father's verbal clumsiness, even amid a crisis. Perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Do your mother and I know that you and Patrick are dating?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And you're both... comfortable with that?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, Dad. Very much so.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, that's... that's wonderful. I mean, he seems like a very nice man. Very successful. And his ex-fiancée, Stevie, she's a lovely...

jokes.text: David, they know, right? / Of course they know! I wouldn't have asked them to come here if I didn't think they knew we were in a relationship! They call the store all the time! I mean, what do they think, I'm just his business partner?! / Possibly. / I mean, yeah.
jokes.setup: David insists Patrick's parents must know about the relationship, spiraling into increasing certainty.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'Possibly' followed by Stevie's 'I mean, yeah' — two characters calmly confirming David's worst fear while he's still talking himself out of it.
jokes.explanation: Beautiful escalation: David's increasingly emphatic rhetorical questions build to a peak, and then Johnny and Stevie deflate it in two beats. 'Possibly' / 'I mean, yeah' is a perfect comedy duo rhythm — understated confirmations that hit harder for how calm they are. The structure of letting David dig his own hole before the laconic response is expertly done.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, your mother and I, we're very supportive of you and Patrick, but we're just trying to understand the dynamic here. You're business partners and you're also... romantically in business together.

jokes.text: Okay, my Dad might've told them, but he, he thought they knew. / And the gift basket that you gave them... had nothing to do with you trying to smooth things over with them?
jokes.setup: Patrick figures out the series of events that led to his parents learning the truth.
jokes.punchline: Patrick connects David's gift basket to damage control — confirming he's read the situation perfectly while David tries to minimize it.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's detective work is charming and the gift basket callback lands as a confirmation that David's gesture was less welcome-gift and more apology-bribe. The rhythm of gentle interrogation suits their dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Romantically in business? That's not a thing.

jokes.text: A few things to expect. Now, the fans will either want a signed picture, a picture and a handshake, or a picture either of you, or with you. / Well, that's reasonable. / Now, if they ask for pictures of your feet...
jokes.setup: The convention handler walks Moira through fan behavior expectations, all of which are completely normal.
jokes.punchline: Mid-sentence, she pivots to feet pictures — treated as a natural continuation of the list — before being cut off, which makes the implication funnier.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the pacing and the interruption. 'Signed picture, handshake, picture' — all expected, reasonable. Then 'if they ask for pictures of your feet' delivered with the same administrative tone. The cut-off before completion is funnier than any completion could be. Structure is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They have to know. They have to know about us.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I mean, what if they find out from someone else? What if they hear it on the street?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We live in a town of 5,000 people. Someone's going to tell them.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And then what? Then I'm a liar. I'm someone who hid this from his own parents.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Do you understand what that makes me?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A bad son. A secretive, deceptive bad son.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So, are you... are you ashamed of me?

jokes.text: Hailey, you have been more than helpful. If I might have a moment alone with my daughter? / Slash publicist, thank you.
jokes.setup: Moira dismisses the handler under the guise of needing privacy.
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately corrects the description — she's not just 'daughter,' she's 'daughter slash publicist' — insisting on professional recognition in the same breath as a personal moment.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's insistence on the professional title is a small but character-rich moment — she's both proud of her new career identity and slightly annoyed to be reduced to just 'daughter.' Efficient and warm.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, yes.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah.

jokes.text: Alexis dear, now is as bad a time as any to tell you. There is a dark side to Mummy's industry, and I've tried to protect you from it your whole life. / You took me to the Playboy Mansion when I was seven. / And you had a wonderful time in the children's grotto.
jokes.setup: Moira begins a solemn speech about protecting Alexis from the dark side of show business.
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately cites the Playboy Mansion at age seven as evidence against this claim; Moira's defense — 'you had a wonderful time in the children's grotto' — implies both that there was a designated children's grotto and that this resolves the concern.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The three-part structure is perfect: (1) Moira's pompous protective-parent framing, (2) Alexis's immediate, specific, devastating counterexample, (3) Moira's defense that 'children's grotto' is a reasonable thing that implies the Playboy Mansion had a designated children's area. The 'children's grotto' detail is extraordinary — it does so much work. It implies the Mansion was so elaborate it had specialized zones, that Moira found this completely acceptable, and that Moira genuinely believes this refutes the concern. This is among the sharpest jokes in the episode. Highly quotable, strong rewatch value.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Everyone needs to stay calm and silent.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What?

jokes.text: Thespians selling their autographs for money in cheerless convention centres. Alexis, you shouldn't have to see this.
jokes.setup: Moira surveys the convention hall with horror.
jokes.punchline: She describes what is a normal, harmless fan convention as something traumatic — 'cheerless convention centres' and the tragic dignity of 'thespians' who sign autographs.
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical horror at something mundane is a reliable bit, but the specific word 'thespians' elevates it — she can't even say 'actors' at a low point. The maternal protection framing ('you shouldn't have to see this') while she's the one who agreed to attend is ironic. Solid Moira moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have protected you from the darkness of this industry.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, you took me to the Playboy Mansion when I was seven.

jokes.text: She only played Grace on 'Touched With Grace' for 37 years. The woman has 17 Emmys for playing a hot-headed nurse who healed people with her sexual touch.
jokes.setup: Moira spots Gloria Gregson and is horrified to be seen.
jokes.punchline: The description of 'Touched With Grace' — 17 Emmys for a nurse who heals with her sexual touch — is a perfect parody of prestige soap opera in one sentence.
jokes.explanation: 'Healed people with her sexual touch' is the key phrase — delivered deadpan as a legitimate Emmy-winning premise. 17 Emmys for this specific role is also absurd. The joke works because Moira delivers it with complete respect, revealing her own value system: this IS prestige television to her.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, there's a children's grotto.

jokes.text: I'm usually the only marquee name at these things. / I'm shocked by my presence as well.
jokes.setup: Gloria warmly says she's glad Moira is here and notes she's usually the only marquee name.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'I'm shocked by my presence as well' — agreeing with the implication that she shouldn't be there while maintaining total dignity.
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect line. 'Shocked by my presence as well' is Moira simultaneously agreeing she doesn't belong (self-aware) and maintaining her dignity by framing it as mutual surprise rather than shame. The economy is exceptional. Highly quotable, strong character integration — this line could only be delivered by Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Gloria Gregson is only the most iconic character in daytime television history. She's a former trapeze artist turned neurologist who discovered a cure for a fictional disease, had an affair with her own son—not biologically, it was very complicated—faked her own death twice, came back as her evil twin, and once performed an emergency C-section on herself using only a letter opener and her wits.

jokes.text: And I am Alexis Rose, Moira's publicist and daughter. / Oh! Daughter? More like sister. Ew.
jokes.setup: Alexis introduces herself to Gloria.
jokes.punchline: Gloria's 'daughter? More like sister' is a flattery-compliment to Moira; Alexis's 'Ew' is a perfect reaction — disgusted at both the implication and the cliché.
jokes.explanation: The 'more like sisters' compliment is a soap opera cliché, and the writers know it — Alexis's disgusted 'Ew' punctures it perfectly. The joke is more in Alexis's reaction than the setup. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Gloria
dialogue.line: Oh honey, with a face like that, I'm surprised your mother didn't leave you at the hospital.

dialogue.speaker: Gloria
dialogue.line: Ew.

jokes.text: I've been piecing together that I don't think I've ever spoken to them outside of work. Is that weird?
jokes.setup: David probes whether Patrick's parents know about the relationship, trying to be casual.
jokes.punchline: David has 'been piecing together' what is actually a glaring gap — a phrase that implies detective work where none should be needed. 'Is that weird?' is understatement of the year.
jokes.explanation: 'Been piecing together' is a funny phrase for something this obvious. The episodic tension makes this land as both comedy and drama. Good cringe setup.

jokes.text: Yeah... / Like, they know about me, right? / ... / Like they know about 'us', right? / Um...
jokes.setup: David's questions escalate in specificity while Patrick's responses dwindle to pauses.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's pregnant 'Um...' in response to 'they know about us, right?' — a silence that lasts several seconds in the transcript and says everything.
jokes.explanation: The comedic/dramatic beat works because of the escalating specificity of David's questions and the inversely shrinking quality of Patrick's answers: 'Yeah... / Like they know about me, right? / [pause] / Like they know about US, right? / Um...' The pause at the end is the episode's best awkward silence. It's simultaneously funny and emotionally loaded. Economy is perfect — nothing is said that doesn't need to be.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I haven't told them because I'm terrified. I'm terrified they won't accept me, I'm terrified they'll reject me, and I'm terrified that everything I've built with David will somehow be less than because of who I am.

jokes.text: That's why I brought this couple home one day in college, and just told my parents to deal with it.
jokes.setup: David is trying to be supportive of Patrick's situation but is compelled to make it about his own experience.
jokes.punchline: David's version of a coming-out support story involves bringing home 'a couple' — not a person — as his self-styled bold disclosure. It's both supportive and self-aggrandizing.
jokes.explanation: Wonderful character moment: David is genuinely trying to be supportive and the story is about courage and parental acceptance — but 'a couple' reveals David's version of bravery is spectacularly maximalist. He couldn't come out with one partner; he needed two. The joke deepens David as a character while getting a laugh. High character integration — this is unmistakably David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know, when I came out to my parents, I was terrified. But they were actually very supportive. So whenever you're ready to tell them about... whoever it is you're seeing, I'm sure they'll be fine with it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Although, I should mention—when I say 'supportive,' I mean they didn't disown me. If you bring home a couple, that might test their limits a bit.

jokes.text: You're gonna laugh yourself right out of the closet on this one.
jokes.setup: David is about to tell Patrick about the surprise party and the parents.
jokes.punchline: 'Laugh yourself right out of the closet' — an unintentional (or intentional) pun that references both the party surprise AND Patrick's literal closeted situation with his parents.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant double-meaning line. On the surface, David is setting up a funny story ('you'll laugh'). But 'out of the closet' in the literal context of Patrick's parents not knowing he's gay is a perfect, devastating pun — especially delivered by David who doesn't seem aware of the full weight. The line works on two levels simultaneously, rewards rewatching, and is highly quotable. One of the top lines in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I need to tell you something. I've been keeping this secret, and I just came out of the closet.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: David, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Literally. I was hiding in the closet to surprise you for your party.

jokes.text: David, I don't think this is how surprise parties work.
jokes.setup: David has just essentially told Patrick there's a surprise party.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's mild, logical observation — delivered with perfect calm — undercuts the moment with gentle irony.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's deadpan is his primary comedic mode. 'I don't think this is how surprise parties work' is technically the most accurate possible response and therefore very funny. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can go back in the closet if that would make you more comfortable.

jokes.text: I could be just your business partner tonight. If that will help.
jokes.setup: David is trying to offer Patrick an out — a way to avoid coming out to his parents at the party.
jokes.punchline: The offer to pretend to be 'just a business partner' after the entire episode has been about the impossibility of that distinction is both sweet and absurd.
jokes.explanation: Callbacks to the 'business partner' running gag while also serving as a genuine emotional beat. The offer is tender and self-effacing, which is funny because it comes from David, who is constitutionally not self-effacing.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Is the party at the cafe?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, I have a prior commitment. I've been... compromised.

jokes.text: David's arrival at the Brewers' motel room with a gift basket — 'we ran out of the body milk, so I threw in some extra lip balms.'
jokes.setup: David shows up at the Brewers' room with a 'welcome present.'
jokes.punchline: 'We ran out of the body milk, so I threw in some extra lip balms' — the specific product detail reveals both David's obsession with his shop's inventory and the slightly-less-than-ideal nature of the apology gift.
jokes.explanation: 'Body milk' is a characteristically David-coded product name, and the casual 'so I threw in some extra lip balms' implies the gift basket is a consolation-prize version of what he intended. The mundane specificity is funny and character-grounded.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I apologize for the miscommunication.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, what exactly did you mean to communicate?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What I said.

jokes.text: Do you think if we'd done things differently, that Patrick would still be... / I understand that this news can come as a shock to some people. But he is still the same person...
jokes.setup: Clint begins asking a question that sounds ominously like it will be homophobic — 'if we'd done things differently, that Patrick would still be...'
jokes.punchline: David preemptively launches into a coming-out defense speech before Clint can finish — and we're with David in the panic. The misdirection resolves when the Brewers reveal they're not upset about Patrick being gay.
jokes.explanation: The episode's most structurally sophisticated comedic beat. Clint's unfinished question ('Patrick would still be...') creates genuine dread that is expertly manipulated — the audience, like David, assumes homophobia. David's preemptive defense speech is character-perfect AND sets up the reversal. When the Brewers reveal they're completely accepting, the relief is both comedic and emotional. This is earned misdirection at a high level — it works dramatically and comedically, and rewards rewatch because Clint's phrasing ('still be') is retrospectively clearly about something else entirely.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're not... upset? About the gay part?

dialogue.speaker: Marci
dialogue.line: Upset? No, honey. We love you.

dialogue.speaker: Clint
dialogue.line: It's everything else about you we can't stand.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, thank God.

jokes.text: David, we're not upset about Patrick being gay. / Oh my God, okay! For a minute I thought this was gonna get very dark. Ahem!
jokes.setup: David has launched into a passionate defense of Patrick and of gay relationships.
jokes.punchline: Marci clarifies they're completely accepting; David's relief explodes into 'Oh my God, okay! For a minute I thought this was gonna get very dark' — breaking character to narrate his own emotional arc.
jokes.explanation: 'For a minute I thought this was gonna get very dark' is David at his most authentically himself — the relief is so great he can't maintain social decorum and instead announces his emotional state like a stage direction. The 'Ahem!' as a self-correction is perfect. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We could just pretend we don't know about it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: ...I see how that sounded.

jokes.text: The thought that Patrick was feeling like he couldn't come and talk to us about this. / Is it because we were so close with Rachel? / Honey, stop!
jokes.setup: The Brewers express hurt that Patrick didn't feel he could come to them.
jokes.punchline: Marci suddenly invokes Rachel — Patrick's ex-fiancée — as a possible reason Patrick felt he couldn't be honest. Clint's immediate 'Honey, stop!' reveals this is a sensitive subject even now.
jokes.explanation: A small comedy beat embedded in an emotional scene — Marci's oversharing impulse ('Is it because we were so close with Rachel?') and Clint's immediate 'Honey, stop!' suggests there's an entire complicated history with Rachel that isn't being unpacked here. Functional character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: We actually used your wine for sangria.

dialogue.speaker: Gloria
dialogue.line: Sangria?! That's a crime against my brand!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, it was cheap sangria...

jokes.text: Hi. / David. Oh, hi. Hi, Clint, Marci. I see you've met my son.
jokes.setup: Patrick arrives at the room to find David already there with his parents.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's flat 'David.' says everything about his recognition that his boyfriend has been managing the situation. Johnny's breezy 'I see you've met my son' — stated as if this is a normal situation — adds to the comedy of everyone pretending normalcy.
jokes.explanation: The comedic value is in the understatement of arrival. Patrick saying only 'David' is a full sentence with subtext. Johnny's normalizing 'I see you've met my son' is funny for its cheerful obliviousness to the situation's complexity.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So you're telling me you won't exploit your extremities for twenty dollars?

dialogue.speaker: Gloria
dialogue.line: Twenty dollars? I charge fifty. And I have to prepare my feet. I can't just show up in sandals.

jokes.text: Patrick is planning on telling you tonight. So I think the best birthday gift we could give him at this point is to just... keep him in the closet until then. I know that came out wrong, but we all understand what I'm saying, right?
jokes.setup: Johnny is coordinating with the Brewers about how to handle the party.
jokes.punchline: 'Keep him in the closet until then' — an accidentally literal use of a figurative phrase, which Johnny immediately catches ('I know that came out wrong') and then nervously checks everyone understood.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. Johnny accidentally uses 'keep him in the closet' literally (meaning 'let him tell them himself') — which is also the figurative phrase for being gay. 'I know that came out wrong' is a secondary pun (came out). The immediate self-correction followed by nervous clarification is perfect Johnny comedy. Highly quotable, strong rewatch value. Note: 'came out wrong' as a self-referential pun in this context is genuinely inspired writing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so I'm thinking we charge $50 for a standard feet photo, $75 if you want her to hold something, and $100 if you want her to do like a pose or a thing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, why would anyone be paying for photographs of my feet?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't know, but apparently there's a market for it. We'd be leaving money on the table if we didn't capitalize on it.

jokes.text: You may have tried my wine, 'Touched With Grapes.' / Oh my God, that's your wine? / We used to use that for sangria, because it was like, so cheap, and... so good.
jokes.setup: Gloria name-drops her wine brand, named after her show 'Touched With Grace.'
jokes.punchline: Alexis recognizes it immediately as the cheap sangria wine — delivering the information with complete innocence, unaware she's delivering an insult.
jokes.explanation: 'Touched With Grapes' as the wine name is already good. Alexis recognizing it as the cheap sangria wine while Gloria beams is delightful. The pause before 'so good' — which is Alexis trying to redeem the comment — doesn't work, which is what makes it great. Alexis's unintentional social disasters are a series staple.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, so we need to coordinate the surprise. Everyone chant 'Surprise!' on three.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's not going to work. Half of you can't count to three.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I can count to three!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Last time we tried this, someone yelled it on two.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Fine. New plan. We just all yell 'Surprise' together.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You're still going to mess it up. We need something simpler.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Like what?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: One word. Just yell 'Hey!' Everyone can handle one word.

jokes.text: So the fiscal sweetener negates any discomfiture one might feel exploiting their extremities for a $20 bill? / $20?! I charge $50. And just in case, I wear open-toed sandals.
jokes.setup: Moira questions whether money justifies selling foot pictures at a convention.
jokes.punchline: Gloria corrects the price upward ($50, not $20) and reveals she came prepared with open-toed sandals — treating foot photography as a professional discipline.
jokes.explanation: Callback to the foot pictures setup, with Gloria delivering the payoff. The price correction (from Moira's $20 assumption to Gloria's $50) establishes foot photography as a legitimate professional tier. 'Just in case, I wear open-toed sandals' is the killer detail — Gloria's professionalism and preparedness for this specific service. Highly quotable. The callback structure between the convention handler's setup and Gloria's execution is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So, how are you enjoying the party?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: By the way, I've been meaning to tell you about this absolutely catastrophic real estate investment I made in 2008.

jokes.text: There is a freedom in letting go, Moira.
jokes.setup: Gloria has been extolling the virtues of convention work.
jokes.punchline: Gloria's philosophical conclusion — 'there is a freedom in letting go' — applied to selling foot pictures at fan conventions is unintentionally (or intentionally) absurd as wisdom.
jokes.explanation: Functional scene-closing wisdom that is funny primarily in context. The pomposity of 'there is a freedom in letting go' applied to convention foot photography is mildly amusing but not a standout.

dialogue.speaker: Clint
dialogue.line: So David, your fashion sense is very... unique.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Clint
dialogue.line: No, I mean — I genuinely don't understand what I'm looking at.

jokes.text: Okay, here's how it's gonna go down. It's 30 seconds each, tickets in hand, or it's back of the line, and no, she will not be clarifying any 'Sunrise Bay' plot points. / Ahem! Feet! / Oh yes. And if we're talking feet, it's $75 for one, $150 for the pair.
jokes.setup: Alexis takes command as publicist/handler for Moira's convention appearance.
jokes.punchline: Moira interrupts with 'Feet!' — demanding the foot pricing be included in the ground rules — and Alexis seamlessly incorporates it at $75/$150.
jokes.explanation: The foot pricing escalation from Gloria's $50 to Moira's $75/$150 is a great callback payoff — Moira has quickly adapted Gloria's business model and inflated the rates. Alexis seamlessly incorporating 'Feet!' into her handler speech without missing a beat is excellent character work. 'She will not be clarifying any Sunrise Bay plot points' is also quietly funny as a specific, professional refusal.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He'll come around. Everyone does. It's only a matter of time before he realizes that I'm right about fashion, and he's been dressing like a cast member from Cops.

jokes.text: He's coming, he's coming! / Okay, he's coming, he's coming. So are we on lights? Also, we're going with 'Surprise.' 'Happy Birthday' was too wordy, and we couldn't nail it at rehearsal.
jokes.setup: Patrick is approaching and the party needs to be ready.
jokes.punchline: They had a rehearsal for saying 'Happy Birthday,' found it too wordy, and are going with just 'Surprise' — David's perfectionist over-preparation applied to the simplest possible task.
jokes.explanation: 'Happy Birthday was too wordy, and we couldn't nail it at rehearsal' is a wonderful detail — David's anxiety about execution has led to rehearsed spontaneity, and even then they couldn't manage four syllables. The phrase 'too wordy' for 'Happy Birthday' is perfectly absurd. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It was one of the happiest nights.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm glad you felt that way, but we really do need to talk about the food waste issue.

jokes.text: Flowers are for you; the pizza's my lunch.
jokes.setup: Patrick arrives at what he thinks is a casual dinner and is greeted by the party.
jokes.punchline: Having accepted the flowers as a birthday gift, Patrick immediately clarifies the pizza is not included — a perfectly calibrated boundary assertion delivered with zero fanfare.
jokes.explanation: A lovely Patrick line — the gift-giving ritual is performed, then immediately qualified. The 'pizza's my lunch' detail implies Patrick was walking around carrying his own lunch when surprised, and that his first instinct amid the chaos is to protect his pizza. Excellent economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: My parents weren't surprised enough when I told them I was getting married. They were just like, 'Oh, that's nice.' So I've come to the conclusion that my parents are just bad actors.

jokes.text: But might I recommend the crab cakes? I over-ordered, and they cost a small fortune.
jokes.setup: David is trying to gracefully step back while Patrick reunites with his parents.
jokes.punchline: Even in this emotionally significant moment, David can't help but try to recover the sunk cost of the crab cakes.
jokes.explanation: David doing emotional-moment grace + crab cake cost anxiety simultaneously is perfect character comedy. 'They cost a small fortune' in a moment of significant personal drama is the right detail. Shows David's emotional economy — he's handling multiple registers at once.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'm just putting together that David's behavior... I'm hoping he at least paid for the gift basket.

jokes.text: I like him. I like him a lot. / Me too. / I don't understand his clothes, but...
jokes.setup: Clint and Marci reassure Patrick they accept David.
jokes.punchline: 'I don't understand his clothes, but...' — an acceptance statement with a gentle, honest qualification that humanizes the Brewers and gets a laugh on the emotional beat.
jokes.explanation: The gentle caveat placed at the emotional peak — 'I don't understand his clothes, but...' — earns a laugh because it's so specific, so true, and so completely acceptable. It lands as both a joke and as warm characterization of the Brewers as regular, well-meaning people. The 'but...' trailing off without completing the concession is good instinct.

jokes.text: Ah, you will in time.
jokes.setup: Clint says he doesn't understand David's clothes.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's confident 'you will in time' — delivered with certainty that his father will come to appreciate David's fashion — is both sweet and subtly funny as a prediction.
jokes.explanation: Four words that do a lot: Patrick loves David's clothes; he's confident his father will come around; and it's funny that David-fashion-acceptance is being treated as an inevitable milestone in their relationship. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: So I know that you're annoyed that the surprise got blown, but this party might go down as one of the happiest nights of my life. / Well, I guess that makes up for the fact that no one ate the crab cakes.
jokes.setup: David reflects warmly on the party's emotional success.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's response cuts through the sentiment with the crab cake complaint — the uneaten crab cakes becoming the symbol of the evening's chaos.
jokes.explanation: A good callback that serves as a tonal balance: after the emotional highs of the coming-out scene, Stevie grounds the episode with the crab cake grievance. The contrast between David's 'happiest night of my life' and Stevie's 'the crab cakes' is the joke. Classic Stevie.

jokes.text: David, I know my parents. They're not good actors. They knew about us, right? / Okay, my Dad might've told them, but he, he thought they knew.
jokes.setup: Patrick pieces together that his parents already knew before he told them.
jokes.punchline: Patrick's calm 'they're not good actors' is a funny way of describing parents who poorly concealed prior knowledge. David's immediate capitulation — 'Okay, my Dad might've told them, but he thought they knew' — is a perfect confession.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's investigative mode is charming and the 'not good actors' framing is a funny way to describe parental transparency. David's multi-qualified confession ('might've,' 'thought they knew') is characteristic damage control. Solid tag-scene comedy.

jokes.text: It was just a very messy day, and I was, I was trying to detangle things, and... and just make everything okay.
jokes.setup: Patrick has caught David's gift-basket bribery scheme.
jokes.punchline: 'Detangle things' as a description of the day's events — gift baskets, surprise parties, outed parents, panic speeches — is a massive understatement delivered with genuine sincerity.
jokes.explanation: 'Detangle things' is a gentle way to describe a chaotic day of interpersonal disasters. David's earnestness saves it. Works as a character moment more than a joke.

jokes.text: It was just a very messy day, and I was, I was trying to detangle things, and just make everything okay. — Yeah. — Well, you made everything okay.
jokes.setup: David attempts to explain himself; his explanation is very David — fussy, self-justifying
jokes.punchline: Patrick simply: 'Well, you made everything okay.' — the understatement lands as both a comedic button and a genuine expression of love
jokes.explanation: The joke is the understatement — Patrick condenses a chaotic, emotionally fraught day into 'well, you made everything okay.' It's warm and funny simultaneously. Not a big laugh but a true one.

jokes.text: Not to step on the moment, but uh, I do have to lock up in five.
jokes.setup: Patrick and David are sharing a warm romantic moment at the end of the episode.
jokes.punchline: Stevie interrupts their tender epilogue with a completely practical concern — locking up — delivered with the same casual tone she'd use for any administrative matter.
jokes.explanation: The perfect episode-closing joke. 'Not to step on the moment, but I do have to lock up in five' is Stevie in pure form — acknowledging she's being a buzzkill while doing it anyway. It pops the romantic balloon without being mean, and the 'in five' is exactly the right time pressure. Strong character integration and economy. A satisfying button.

tmdb_overview: Patrick’s parents are in town for his surprise party, but the parents end up with an even bigger surprise, while Alexis drags Moira to a soap opera convention.
insight: Family awkwardness and David's restrained panic drive a solid but uneven character-comedy episode.
